---
title: "G-Computation Workshop Demo"
subtitle: "Interactive code cells powered by WebR"
format: html
engine: knitr
filters:
  - webr
webr:
  show-startup-message: false
  packages:
    - ggplot2
---

This is a proof-of-concept demonstrating interactive R code cells in the browser.
No R installation required — code runs via WebAssembly using [WebR](https://docs.r-wasm.org/webr/latest/).

## Try it: G-Computation Step by Step

### Step 1: Simulate trial data

Edit the code below and click **Run Code** to execute. Try changing the sample size or the treatment effect.

```{webr-r}
# Simulate a simple RCT-like dataset
set.seed(2026)
n <- 500

W1 <- rnorm(n)              # confounder: age (standardized)
W2 <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.4)     # confounder: sex
A  <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(-0.5 + 0.3 * W1 + 0.2 * W2))  # treatment
Y  <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(-1.5 + 0.6 * A + 0.8 * W1 + 0.3 * W2))  # outcome

dat <- data.frame(W1, W2, A, Y)
cat("n =", n, "\n")
cat("Treated:", sum(A), " Control:", sum(1-A), "\n")
cat("Outcome rate (treated):", round(mean(Y[A==1]), 3), "\n")
cat("Outcome rate (control):", round(mean(Y[A==0]), 3), "\n")
```

### Step 2: Fit the outcome model

```{webr-r}
# Fit logistic regression for E[Y | A, W]
mod <- glm(Y ~ A + W1 + W2, family = binomial, data = dat)
round(coef(mod), 3)
```

### Step 3: Predict counterfactual outcomes

This is the core of G-computation — predict what would happen to *everyone* under treatment and under control.

```{webr-r}
# Counterfactual predictions
dat1 <- dat; dat1$A <- 1  # everyone treated
dat0 <- dat; dat0$A <- 0  # everyone untreated

p1 <- predict(mod, newdata = dat1, type = "response")
p0 <- predict(mod, newdata = dat0, type = "response")

risk1 <- mean(p1)
risk0 <- mean(p0)
ate   <- risk1 - risk0

cat("E[Y(1)] =", round(risk1, 4), "\n")
cat("E[Y(0)] =", round(risk0, 4), "\n")
cat("ATE     =", round(ate, 4), "(risk difference)\n")
cat("RR      =", round(risk1/risk0, 3), "\n")
```

### Step 4: Explore — what happens if you change the model?

Try adding an interaction term (`A:W1`) or removing a confounder. How does the ATE change?

```{webr-r}
# Try a different model specification
mod2 <- glm(Y ~ A * W1 + W2, family = binomial, data = dat)

p1_v2 <- predict(mod2, newdata = dat1, type = "response")
p0_v2 <- predict(mod2, newdata = dat0, type = "response")

cat("Original ATE:", round(ate, 4), "\n")
cat("With interaction ATE:", round(mean(p1_v2) - mean(p0_v2), 4), "\n")
```

### Step 5: Visualize the propensity score

```{webr-r}
ps_mod <- glm(A ~ W1 + W2, family = binomial, data = dat)
dat$ps <- predict(ps_mod, type = "response")

# Simple histogram of propensity scores by treatment group
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
hist(dat$ps[dat$A == 1], breaks = 20, main = "Treated",
     xlab = "Propensity Score", col = "#228833AA", xlim = c(0, 1))
hist(dat$ps[dat$A == 0], breaks = 20, main = "Control",
     xlab = "Propensity Score", col = "#EE6677AA", xlim = c(0, 1))
```

---

## How this works

- Code runs entirely **in your browser** via WebAssembly (no server needed)
- You can edit any code cell and re-run it
- Results update in real time
- Base R and select packages (ggplot2, etc.) are available
- Complex packages (tmle, SuperLearner, lmtp) are not yet available in WebR

For the full static handbook with all chapters and pre-computed results, see the [main site](../index.html).
